result:
The rain doesn’t fall in straight lines here. It comes sideways, lazy and mean, the way an old boxer throws a jab he knows will land. I’m sitting on the warped wooden steps of a rented house that smells permanently of cedar and someone else’s regret, watching water bead on the leaves of a dying monstera someone left behind. The plant is winning the war against neglect in small, stubborn ways. I respect that.

There’s a particular silence that arrives after midnight in places like this—halfway between mountain and sea, where the wind forgets which direction it’s supposed to be going. The kind of silence that makes you wonder if the world paused just to see what you’d do with all that empty space between heartbeats. I’ve been filling mine with bad coffee and worse decisions, which feels honest if nothing else.

Yesterday I found an old typewriter in the attic. Not one of those sleek mid-century numbers that hipsters pay too much for. This one is ugly. Olive-green, heavy as guilt, with keys that stick on the letters Q and M like they’re personally offended by those sounds. The ribbon is dried out but still smells like ink and 1973. I brought it down anyway. Set it on the kitchen table like a gauntlet. So far it has won. I haven’t typed a single word.

Instead I’ve been thinking about how we romanticize the wrong things. We talk about the romance of train stations and rainy cities and men who smoke on balconies, but nobody ever mentions the particular beauty of a grocery store at 1:17 a.m. when you’re the only person there who isn’t stocking shelves. The way the fluorescent lights buzz like distant bees. How the produce mist makes everything look like it’s been crying. The tired cashier who calls you “hon” even though she’s twenty-three and you’re old enough to be her disappointing uncle. There’s holiness in that fluorescent loneliness if you’re brave enough to look straight at it.

I’ve been reading old letters I never sent. Not metaphorical ones. Actual letters. Written on hotel stationery from cities I barely remember visiting. One is dated 2016 from a Marriott in Pittsburgh. It begins “Dear E—” and then stops, as if the act of writing her name used up all the courage I had that night. The rest of the page is blank except for a coffee ring that looks like a halo that gave up. I don’t remember what I wanted to say anymore. That’s probably merciful.

There’s a fox that’s been coming around at twilight. Not the sleek Instagram foxes people post with captions about spirit animals. This one looks like it lost a fight with a lawnmower and then lost another fight with depression. Its left ear is shredded. It walks with the careful dignity of something that knows exactly how ridiculous it looks but has decided to commit anyway. I leave sardines out sometimes. We have an understanding. I don’t try to befriend it and it doesn’t pretend to trust me. It’s the most honest relationship I’ve had in years.

I’ve been thinking about scale lately. How the same day can feel both endless and impossibly brief. How a single sentence can rearrange the architecture of a life. How the universe is 13.8 billion years old and yet here I am, forty-three years old, getting emotionally invested in the survival of a half-dead houseplant and a fox that probably has rabies. The absurdity is comforting. If everything is ridiculous, then nothing has to matter more than it can bear.

The typewriter is still staring at me. I think it knows I’m scared of it. Not of the machine itself, but of what might come out if I actually touch the keys. There are sentences living in me that I’ve been avoiding for decades. They have sharp edges and they know where all my soft places are. Sometimes at 3 a.m. I can feel them moving around like animals in the walls. Scratching. Patient.

I’ve started naming the shadows in the house. There’s one in the hallway that looks like a priest who changed his mind. Another in the corner of the bedroom that might be my father at thirty or might be me at eighty. They don’t speak, which is decent of them. I’ve had enough of ghosts who won’t shut up.

The rain has stopped. The monstera leaf closest to the window is trembling like it’s trying to decide whether this reprieve is real or just the storm catching its breath. I know how it feels.

I think I’m going to type something now. Not because I have something important to say, but because the not-saying has become heavier than the saying could ever be. The Q key will stick. The M will fight me. The page will probably end up in the woodstove. But for one moment the hammer will hit the ribbon and something will be made that wasn’t there before.

That feels like enough reason to try.

The fox is watching from the tree line. I can see its eyes reflecting the kitchen light—two small dishonest moons. It looks like it’s laughing at me, which is fair. I’d laugh at me too.

But I’m going to type anyway.

The first sentence is always the hardest. After that it’s just gravity and consequence and the particular way ink smells when it’s fresh on paper. Some things are worth the mess they make.

I think I’ll start with the rain. Not because it’s profound, but because it’s falling again, harder this time, and the sound it makes on the metal roof sounds exactly like every apology I never gave anyone.

Close enough.

(Word count: 1002)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
